patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
[PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
[PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the number of iterations in the shrink_delalloc function to prevent it from blocking indefinitely when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function now iterates over delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim all available space at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrarily long syscall latencies.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64
[PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64
[PATCH v4 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch changes the type of `outstanding_extents` in the Btrfs inode structure from a 32-bit unsigned integer to a 64-bit unsigned integer (`u64`). This is done because the maximum file size, defined by `MAX_LFS_FILESIZE`, can result in extent sizes that exceed the bounds of a 32-bit unsigned integer. The patch also updates related functions and includes additional assertions to ensure correctness.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v4 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where delayed reference reservations were under-estimated due to compression. When compression is enabled, the maximum extent size is reduced from 128MiB to 128KiB, leading to a significant discrepancy between estimated and actual extents. The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_inode_max_extents` to calculate the correct number of extents based on the inode's properties and the maximum extent size. This change ensures that reservations are accurately calculated even when compression is enabled, preventing under-reservation of delayed reference reservations for delalloc writes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v4 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem in the Btrfs file system where space is not reserved for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but this calculation does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic. The patch aims to fix this issue by reserving space for delayed refs during delalloc operations.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v4 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses stalls caused by sudden writeback on systems with large memory and disk sizes. The patches improve reservations for delayed refs in delalloc, reduce the size of work units in shrink_delalloc(), and make some basic smoothing changes to flush_space(). This is a targeted approach to mitigate issues related to metadata space system tailspins and user stalls waiting for ordered extents and delayed refs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] userfaultfd: preserve write protection across UFFDIO_MOVE

version_history.subject: [PATCH] userfaultfd: preserve write protection across UFFDIO_MOVE

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the userfaultfd code to preserve write protection when moving pages using UFFDIO_MOVE. The original intent was to follow mremap() behavior, but it was found that mremap()'s move_ptes() preserves the source write state unconditionally. The patch adds checks for uffd-wp and write protection before setting the destination PTE writable.

review_comments.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch by adding a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Apr 9, 2026 at 8:28 AM Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:
>
> move_present_ptes() unconditionally makes the destination PTE writable,
> dropping uffd-wp write-protection from the source PTE.
>
> The original intent was to follow mremap() behavior, but mremap()'s
> move_ptes() preserves the source write state unconditionally.
>
> Modify uffd to preserve the source write state and check the uffd-wp
> condition of the source before setting writable on the destination.
>
> Fixes: adef440691ba ("userfaultfd: UFFDIO_MOVE uABI")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

Reviewed-by: Suren Baghdasaryan <surenb@google.com>

> ---
>  mm/userfaultfd.c | 5 ++++-
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> index e6dfd5f28acd..783ca68aed88 100644
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -1123,7 +1123,10 @@ static long move_present_ptes(struct mm_struct *mm,
>                         orig_dst_pte = pte_mksoft_dirty(orig_dst_pte);
>                 if (pte_dirty(orig_src_pte))
>                         orig_dst_pte = pte_mkdirty(orig_dst_pte);
> -               orig_dst_pte = pte_mkwrite(orig_dst_pte, dst_vma);
> +               if (pte_write(orig_src_pte))
> +                       orig_dst_pte = pte_mkwrite(orig_dst_pte, dst_vma);
> +               if (pte_uffd_wp(orig_src_pte))
> +                       orig_dst_pte = pte_mkuffd_wp(orig_dst_pte);
>                 set_pte_at(mm, dst_addr, dst_pte, orig_dst_pte);
>
>                 src_addr += PAGE_SIZE;
> --
> 2.52.0
>

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch by adding a Reviewed-by tag, but noted that it's still under review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Apr 09, 2026 at 11:28:22AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> move_present_ptes() unconditionally makes the destination PTE writable,
> dropping uffd-wp write-protection from the source PTE.
> 
> The original intent was to follow mremap() behavior, but mremap()'s
> move_ptes() preserves the source write state unconditionally.
> 
> Modify uffd to preserve the source write state and check the uffd-wp
> condition of the source before setting writable on the destination.
> 
> Fixes: adef440691ba ("userfaultfd: UFFDIO_MOVE uABI")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

Reviewed-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

> ---
>  mm/userfaultfd.c | 5 ++++-
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> index e6dfd5f28acd..783ca68aed88 100644
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -1123,7 +1123,10 @@ static long move_present_ptes(struct mm_struct *mm,
>  			orig_dst_pte = pte_mksoft_dirty(orig_dst_pte);
>  		if (pte_dirty(orig_src_pte))
>  			orig_dst_pte = pte_mkdirty(orig_dst_pte);
> -		orig_dst_pte = pte_mkwrite(orig_dst_pte, dst_vma);
> +		if (pte_write(orig_src_pte))
> +			orig_dst_pte = pte_mkwrite(orig_dst_pte, dst_vma);
> +		if (pte_uffd_wp(orig_src_pte))
> +			orig_dst_pte = pte_mkuffd_wp(orig_dst_pte);
>  		set_pte_at(mm, dst_addr, dst_pte, orig_dst_pte);
>  
>  		src_addr += PAGE_SIZE;
> -- 
> 2.52.0
> 

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/3] pull region-specific logic into new files

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/3] cxl/core/region: move pmem region driver logic into region_pmem.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the logic for managing Persistent Memory (PMEM) regions from the overloaded region.c file into a new, dedicated region_pmem.c file. This separation makes it clear that this code only applies to PMEM regions and helps to declutter the original file. The move is done without introducing any functional changes, making it a refactoring effort aimed at improving maintainability and organization of the codebase.

review_comments.author: Ira Weiny
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/3] cxl/core/region: move dax region device logic into region_dax.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the CXL DAX region device logic from the overloaded region.c file into a new region_dax.c file, allowing for additional dax-driver integration paths without further dirtying the core region.c logic. The move includes the creation of a new region_dax.c file and modifications to the Makefile, core.h, and region.c files. No functional changes are made as part of this patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE
[PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: kick writeback flusher instead of inline flush for IOCB_DONTCACHE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path. Instead of submitting writeback inline, it uses a background flusher thread to handle writeback asynchronously and coalescence I/O without explicit skip-if-busy or dirty pressure checks. The patch introduces a new function `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` that wakes up the BDI flusher thread with a specific reason for tracing visibility.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ritesh pointed out that the new API `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` gives a misleading sense of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective BDI. He suggested exporting the symbol for `wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()` and using it instead, or modifying the API to make its purpose clearer.
review_comments.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current implementation of filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback causes a WB_SYNC_NONE writeback every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is not desired, and suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit to handle this case.
review_comments.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig suggested that the new WB_REASON_DONTCACHE reason for tracing visibility should not be exposed to userspace, and also requested that the function wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() be marked as static in fs-writeback.c.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Jan Kara noted that the patch could lead to performance regressions if a process creates a large temporary file and deletes it shortly after creation, while another process does small DONTCACHE writes; he suggested this behavior would cause unnecessary allocation, writeback, and deletion of the temporary file

Reviewer Jan Kara expressed concerns about how the patch would handle writeback for inodes with dropbehind set, suggesting either tracking folios per inode or lazily clearing a flag once the inode is clean.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is all true and I can certainly construct a workload where this
behavior will lead to big regressions in performance - for example one
process creating say 1G temporary file and deleting it shortly after
creation + one process doing small DONTCACHE writes. Before this change
only DONTCACHE writes will make it to the disk and temporary file will only
be in the page cache, after this change you pay for allocating, writing
back, and deleting the temporary file.

---

But I'm not sure how you imagine this would work without restricting
writeback to particular inodes. Maybe we could mark inodes which have
folios with dropbehind set and make flush worker only write such inodes?
That would probably require tracking number of folios with dropbehind flag
per inode but that wouldn't be too bad. Or we could just lazily clear this
"inode has dropbehind folios" flag once the inode is clean.

								Honza

-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested that instead of waking up the flusher thread to drain all dirty pages, it should only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios, which would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios.
It would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the new writeback mechanism may cause dontcache folios to stay cached for a long time if mixed with normal writes, but agreed that it might level out over longer periods.
review_comments.raw_body:
So that may result in dontcache folios staying cached for quite some time
if DONTCACHE and normal writes are mixed. But I agree over longer time it
might just level out so maybe that's fine.

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that IOCB_DONTCACHE on writes doesn't provide guarantees about when the writeback will be done, and clarified that it only means 'don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback'.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think that's probably OK. In my interpretation, DONTCACHE on write
just means "don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback".
It doesn't provide any guarantees about when the writeback will be
done.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite, nfsd-io-bench, to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and libnfs against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes various workloads such as sequential and random read/write, multi-writer, noisy-neighbor, and latency-sensitive reader tests. It also includes scripts for setting up the NFS export, running the benchmarks, and parsing the results from fio's JSON output. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. This patch aims to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for specific I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the performance of the per-inode writeback approach, specifically that it regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads due to lost I/O batching. The author acknowledges this regression and suggests sticking with v2, which has a different approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, that version regressed performance in a couple of cases. I think v2 is probably the best approach, on balance. Maybe we can get this into -next so that it can make v7.2?

Here's the comparison between this version and a writeback_single_inode() flush version:

------------------8<-----------------------

● Comparing dontcache numbers against the previous whole-BDI flusher kernel (from /tmp/dontcache-local-4way-flusher.md):                                                                          

  Per-Inode vs Whole-BDI Flusher — DONTCACHE on Local XFS                                                                                                                                         

  Single-Client Writes

  ┌──────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐                                                                                                                                      
  │    Benchmark     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │                                                                                                                                      
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤      
  │ Seq write MB/s   │ 1450      │ 1438      │ -1% (noise) │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Seq write p99.9  │ 23.5 ms   │ 23.5 ms   │ identical   │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write MB/s  │ 363       │ 286       │ -21%        │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write p99.9 │ 1.8 ms    │ 16.7 ms   │ regression  │
  └──────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Seq write is identical. Rand write regressed — the whole-BDI flusher batched all dirty pages in one pass with writeback_sb_inodes() under a single blk_plug, while per-inode write_inode_now() loses that batching.

  Single-Client Reads

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────┐
  │   Benchmark    │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │ Change │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Seq read MB/s  │ 2950      │ 2350      │ -20%   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Rand read MB/s │ 651       │ 519       │ -20%   │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────┘

  Reads shouldn't be affected by writeback path changes. Buffered reads also dropped (2888 → 2331), suggesting different system conditions between runs rather than a per-inode regression.

  Multi-Writer (Scenario A)

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┐
  │     Metric     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ Aggregate MB/s │ 1478      │ 999       │ -32%       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ p99.9          │ 46 ms     │ 77 ms     │ -67% worse │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┘

  This is the biggest regression. With whole-BDI, the flusher did one batched pass through all dirty inodes via writeback_sb_inodes(). With per-inode, each of 4 writers queues a separate work item processed serially by write_inode_now() — losing the batch I/O merging benefit.

  Scenario C & D (Noisy Neighbor)

  ┌─────────────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐
  │         Metric          │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C writer       │ 1468      │ 1386      │ -6%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C readers      │ 18.7 MB/s │ 18.7 MB/s │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D writer       │ 1472      │ 1467      │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D readers      │ 496 MB/s  │ 507 MB/s  │ +2%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D reader p99.9 │ 440 us    │ 358 us    │ +19% better │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Mixed-mode (Scenario D) is the intended production case and it's essentially identical or slightly better — per-inode writeback creates less device contention for buffered readers.

  Summary

  The per-inode approach is neutral-to-slightly-better for the production scenario (Scenario D), but regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads. The core issue is loss of I/O batching
 — writeback_sb_inodes() processes all dirty inodes in one blk_plug'd pass, while per-inode write_inode_now() calls are processed one at a time. The read regressions likely reflect different
  system conditions since buffered/direct reads also dropped ~20%.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Ritesh pointed out that the current API gives a misleading impression of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space mapping, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective bdi. He suggested exporting the symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() and using it instead, or alternatively modifying filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason) to better reflect its behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the current implementation of wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi causes WB_SYNC_NONE writeback to occur every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is inefficient and unnecessary. He suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper function, and other related changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig requested that the extra reason arguments for wide wakeups not be exposed outside the core writeback code, and also suggested marking wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi static in fs-writeback.c
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the benchmark results provided in the patch description are incomplete and potentially stale, suggesting they be included directly in the patch for better reproducibility. Additionally, he questioned the validity of certain benchmark numbers, specifically the read changes between patches and unpatched dontcache in XFS and NFSD, requesting further investigation into their causes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please add the results into the patch.  Besides having them in the patch
vs the cover letter, URLs to random hosting sites tend to get stale
very easily.

Comments on the XFS numbers / benchmark setup:

How does O_DIRECT manage to create almost the same peek dirty as
buffered?  Similarly even this patched doncache show be lower, which
feels odd.

(the editorializing in these results feels odd, not sure what tool
generates them, but maybe edit it for the commit log to look a bit more
serious).

Given that this patch does not change the read path, how do the pure read
numbers for patches vs unpatched dontcache manage to differ so much?

Comments on the NFSD numbers / benchmark setup:

I though you were testing using dontcache on the server, but the
comments seem to imply it is done on the client, what is the case
here? 

Again, the read changes between patches and unpatched look suspect.
Please drill down why they happen, the fact that patches is slower here
and faster in XFS makes me wonder if the results might just be very
volatile?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that limiting writeback to a specific inode has several drawbacks, which explains the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE in write-heavy workloads.

Reviewer expressed concern about the timing of the patchset's submission, noting that targeting 7.2 is not a priority and that the merge window for 7.1 is closing soon, also requesting input from Jens
review_comments.raw_body:
Limiting writeback to a specific inode has many downsides as I explained
before, so this is not surprising.

---

If you want to target 7.2 we're not in any rush.  If you were trying to
say 7.1 we're way to close too the merge window.  We also haven't
heard from Jens at all, whom I'd really like to look over this.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Jan Kara noted that the patch can lead to performance regressions under certain workloads, specifically when a process creates a large temporary file and deletes it shortly after creation while another process does small DONTCACHE writes. This results in unnecessary allocation, writeback, and deletion of the temporary file.

Reviewer Jan Kara expressed concerns about the patch's approach to writeback and suggested two alternative solutions: either marking inodes with dropbehind set and restricting flush worker to only write such inodes, or lazily clearing an 'inode has dropbehind folios' flag once the inode is clean.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is all true and I can certainly construct a workload where this
behavior will lead to big regressions in performance - for example one
process creating say 1G temporary file and deleting it shortly after
creation + one process doing small DONTCACHE writes. Before this change
only DONTCACHE writes will make it to the disk and temporary file will only
be in the page cache, after this change you pay for allocating, writing
back, and deleting the temporary file.

---

But I'm not sure how you imagine this would work without restricting
writeback to particular inodes. Maybe we could mark inodes which have
folios with dropbehind set and make flush worker only write such inodes?
That would probably require tracking number of folios with dropbehind flag
per inode but that wouldn't be too bad. Or we could just lazily clear this
"inode has dropbehind folios" flag once the inode is clean.

								Honza

-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested modifying the patch to expedite writes by the number of pending don't-cache folios, which would still prioritize writing the least recently dirtied inodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios.
It would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara noted that the patch may cause dontcache folios to stay cached for a long time if mixed with normal writes, but agreed that it might level out over longer periods.
review_comments.raw_body:
So that may result in dontcache folios staying cached for quite some time
if DONTCACHE and normal writes are mixed. But I agree over longer time it
might just level out so maybe that's fine.

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed Jan Kara's concern that RWF_DONTCACHE on writes provides guarantees about when the writeback will be done, explaining it only means 'don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback'. No fix is planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think that's probably OK. In my interpretation, DONTCACHE on write
just means "don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback".
It doesn't provide any guarantees about when the writeback will be
done.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC][PATCH] make sure that lock_for_kill() callers drop the locks in safe order
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch ensures that callers of the lock_for_kill() function properly drop locks in a safe order to prevent potential deadlocks or hangs.

review_comments.author: Calvin Owens
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Calvin Owens reported that a reproducer he provided causes the system to hang in an infinite loop in d_walk() when using a stock 7.0-rc7 kernel, and suggested that this is not related to Al's livelock issue because only one thread is spinning on an otherwise idle machine.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Jeff,

I've been poking at this one too. With the attached reproducer, the
waiter will get stuck in an infinite loop apparently in d_walk() with a
stock 7.0-rc7 (in my case with PREEMPT_LAZY):

        d_walk
        list_empty (inlined)
        shrink_dcache_tree
        d_invalidate
        proc_invalidate_siblings_dcache
        release_task
        wait_task_zombie (inlined)
        wait_consider_task
        do_wait_thread (inlined)
        __do_wait
        do_wait
        kernel_wait4
        __do_sys_wait4 (inlined)
        __se_sys_wait4 (inlined)
        __x64_sys_wait4
        do_syscall_64
        entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe
        __internal_syscall_cancel
        __syscall_cancel
        __wait4 (inlined)
        os_wait_impl (inlined)
        os_wait
        _Py_LeaveRecursiveCallTstate (inlined)
        cfunction_vectorcall_NOARGS
        _PyObject_VectorcallTstate (inlined)
        PyObject_Vectorcall

It doesn't happen 100% of the time, but it's never taken more than three
attempts on two different machines. I've SIGKILL'd it and waited over an
hour, it really never comes back. Making NR_FORKS larger than 3 was
counterproductive on my 32-thread machines, but YMMV of course.

I haven't had time to dig more yet, but wanted to share the reproducer,
hopefully it yields something useful on your end.

I don't think this is Al's livelock, because only one thread is
spinning on an otherwise idle machine... but I apologize if it is and
it's going over my head.

Cheers,
Calvin

---
#!/usr/bin/python3
# sudo prlimit --nofile=10000000:10000000 ./repro.py

import os

NR_FORKS = 3

for _ in range(NR_FORKS):
	pid = os.fork()
	if pid == 0:
		break

for dirpath, _, filenames in os.walk("/proc"):
	for fn in filenames:
		try:
			os.open(os.path.join(dirpath, fn), os.O_RDONLY)
		except:
			pass

if pid == 0:
	os._exit(0)

while True:
	try:
		os.wait()
	except ChildProcessError:
		os._exit(0)

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro asked Calvin Owens to check if a specific Git repository branch reproduces the issue, indicating that he wants to verify whether the problem is consistent across different setups.
review_comments.raw_body:
Interesting...  I'll try it in a bit, but just in case - could you check if
git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/viro/vfs.git #work.dcache-busy-wait
steps into the same thing on your setup?

review_comments.author: Calvin Owens
review_comments.summary: Calvin Owens noted that the infinite spins issue is related to 'magic' symlinks in /proc/$/fd/*, and provided a patch (fs/proc: Implement d_weak_revalidate()) that makes it impossible to trigger the hang with his reproducer.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Al,

It still reproduces on that branch in the exact same way.

I'm absolutely 100% guessing... but I recall tomfoolery with the "magic"
symlinks in /proc/$/fd/* in the past... the below patch makes the infinite
spins impossible to trigger with my reproducer, does my explanation make
any sense to you? Apologies if I'm off in the weeds...

Thanks,
Calvin

---
From: Calvin Owens <calvin@wbinvd.org>
Subject: [PATCH] fs/proc: Implement d_weak_revalidate()

I think this is necessary because ND_JUMPED can happen in /proc when
the magic symlinks in /proc/$/fd/* point to files in /proc. If true,
this is a problem, because the default behavior for d_weak_revalidate()
is to assume the jumped-to dentries are always valid.

Fix by checking the in_use count in d_weak_revalidate() for procfs, the
same way it is checked in procfs d_revalidate() today.

Signed-off-by: Calvin Owens <calvin@wbinvd.org>
---
 fs/proc/generic.c | 22 +++++++++++++++++-----
 1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/proc/generic.c b/fs/proc/generic.c
index 501889856461..359176e5eeb3 100644
--- a/fs/proc/generic.c
+++ b/fs/proc/generic.c
@@ -216,15 +216,26 @@ void proc_free_inum(unsigned int inum)
 	ida_free(&proc_inum_ida, inum - PROC_DYNAMIC_FIRST);
 }
 
+static int __proc_misc_revalidate(struct dentry *dentry)
+{
+	if (atomic_read(&PDE(d_inode(dentry))->in_use) < 0)
+		return 0; /* revalidate */
+	return 1;
+}
+
 static int proc_misc_d_revalidate(struct inode *dir, const struct qstr *name,
 				  struct dentry *dentry, unsigned int flags)
 {
 	if (flags & LOOKUP_RCU)
 		return -ECHILD;
 
-	if (atomic_read(&PDE(d_inode(dentry))->in_use) < 0)
-		return 0; /* revalidate */
-	return 1;
+	return __proc_misc_revalidate(dentry);
+}
+
+static int proc_misc_d_weak_revalidate(struct dentry * dentry,
+				       unsigned int flags)
+{
+	return __proc_misc_revalidate(dentry);
 }
 
 static int proc_misc_d_delete(const struct dentry *dentry)
@@ -233,8 +244,9 @@ static int proc_misc_d_delete(const struct dentry *dentry)
 }
 
 static const struct dentry_operations proc_misc_dentry_ops = {
-	.d_revalidate	= proc_misc_d_revalidate,
-	.d_delete	= proc_misc_d_delete,
+	.d_revalidate		= proc_misc_d_revalidate,
+	.d_weak_revalidate	= proc_misc_d_weak_revalidate,
+	.d_delete		= proc_misc_d_delete,
 };
 
 /*
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro noted that the patch's test case is causing a livelock or UAF in dcache due to threads opening and exiting files in /proc/*, leading to invalidation of /proc/* and triggering unnecessary cleanup. He suggested that the mix of files being opened was unpredictable and requested further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
What it's doing, AFAICS, is a weird way of opening arseloads of files, mostly
the same ones again and again - the reason it needs that prlimit (there's
way fewer files in procfs when it starts) is that these threads keep running
through each other's /proc/*/fd and opening what they'd got again and again,
adding to /proc/*/fd/* as they go.

IOW, you are opening a random mix of files, both procfs and ones some processes
had opened, then exiting.  That triggers invalidation of your /proc/*, with
a _lot_ of shite that needs to be taken out.  It might be triggering a livelock
or a UAF somewhere in dcache or it might be something entirely different -
no idea at that point.  I'll look further into that, but I wouldn't be surprised
if it turns out to be entirely unrelated.  Would be easier to deal with if the
mix had been more predictable, but we have what we have...

review_comments.author: Calvin Owens
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Calvin Owens noted that a pathological behavior involving d_walk() spin consistently triggered when the reproducer didn't ignore itself in /proc, and suspected the magic symlinks as the cause.

Reviewer Calvin Owens expressed appreciation for the patch author's efforts and offered to test additional reproducer configurations, indicating that the issue is consistent on two different machines.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes exactly, I was trying a lot of different pathological behaviors and
that was the first pattern I found that triggered the d_walk() spin
consistently.

Initially I made the reproducer ignore itself in /proc. But it turns out
to be much more reliable if it *doesn't*. I realized it was opening its
own children's files in /proc/*/fd/*, and the children were also opening
other /proc files, which is what made me suspect the magic symlinks.

---

I'm sure I can narrow down the reproducer more, I'll try a bit.

I appreciate you taking a look. I'm happy to test anything else you like
if that reproducer isn't working for you, it's very consistent for me on
two different machines. Hopefully it works for Jeff.

Thanks,
Calvin

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro demonstrated a pathological case where d_invalidate() gets stuck in an infinite loop due to a large number of busy dentries, and suggested that the patch should not use shrink_dcache_parent() in d_invalidate().

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC][PATCH] make sure that lock_for_kill() callers drop the locks in safe order
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch ensures that callers of the lock_for_kill() function properly drop locks in a safe order to prevent potential deadlocks or hangs.

review_comments.author: Paul McKenney
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that in a preempt-disabled kernel, the order of rcu_read_unlock() and spin_unlock() does not matter due to preemption being entirely disabled
sentiment_signals: no specific technical concern or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you, Jeff!

Yes that has preemption entirely disabled, so RCU readers do nothing but
disable and enable preemption.  Except that it is everywhere disabled,
so the order of the rcu_read_unlock() and the spin_unlock() should
not matter.  (Famous last words!)

							Thanx, Paul

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC][PATCH] make sure that lock_for_kill() callers drop the locks in safe order
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch ensures that callers of the lock_for_kill() function properly drop locks in a safe order to prevent potential deadlocks or hangs.

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Al Viro expressed concern that the mdelay() call in the reproducer attempt may mask the underlying issue being hunted, and requested its removal to better understand the problem.

Reviewer expressed skepticism about the patch's ability to reproduce a UAF, citing that even with an mdelay() inserted after cond_resched(), the hang could still be reproduced, and questioned whether the patch addresses the root cause of the issue.
sentiment_signals: concern about potential masking of underlying issue
review_comments.raw_body:
Elimination of busy-wait is likely to hide whatever's going on, so it
shouldn't be there when hunting for reproducer.

---

Not even with mdelay() stuck in there?  FWIW, another UAF report in the
same general area is parisc buildd running into that when building
a specific package (webkit2gtk); Debian kernels 6.16..6.19 (at least),
no idea which config it is...

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that reproducing the use-after-free (UAF) issue was challenging, citing multiple attempts by himself and Gustavo MacIntosh Alves Santos, and noted that only hangs were reproducible.

Author acknowledged Al Viro's concern about the mdelay() preventing dentry freeing progress, and is experimenting with a random delay to reproduce the UAF.
review_comments.raw_body:
This was before that. I know Gustavo also tried to reproduce it and he
was able to hit the hang but no UAF.

---

Yep, not even with that. One thing that claude pointed out: doing an
mdelay() might prevent it from making any progress freeing dentries.
I'm experimenting now with one that only randomly does a delay, but so
far that hasn't turned up anything. I'll let you know if that changes.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Calvin Owens
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Calvin Owens reported that a reproducer he provided causes the system to hang in an infinite loop in d_walk() when using a stock 7.0-rc7 kernel, and suggested that this is not related to Al's livelock issue because only one thread is spinning on an otherwise idle machine.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Jeff,

I've been poking at this one too. With the attached reproducer, the
waiter will get stuck in an infinite loop apparently in d_walk() with a
stock 7.0-rc7 (in my case with PREEMPT_LAZY):

        d_walk
        list_empty (inlined)
        shrink_dcache_tree
        d_invalidate
        proc_invalidate_siblings_dcache
        release_task
        wait_task_zombie (inlined)
        wait_consider_task
        do_wait_thread (inlined)
        __do_wait
        do_wait
        kernel_wait4
        __do_sys_wait4 (inlined)
        __se_sys_wait4 (inlined)
        __x64_sys_wait4
        do_syscall_64
        entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe
        __internal_syscall_cancel
        __syscall_cancel
        __wait4 (inlined)
        os_wait_impl (inlined)
        os_wait
        _Py_LeaveRecursiveCallTstate (inlined)
        cfunction_vectorcall_NOARGS
        _PyObject_VectorcallTstate (inlined)
        PyObject_Vectorcall

It doesn't happen 100% of the time, but it's never taken more than three
attempts on two different machines. I've SIGKILL'd it and waited over an
hour, it really never comes back. Making NR_FORKS larger than 3 was
counterproductive on my 32-thread machines, but YMMV of course.

I haven't had time to dig more yet, but wanted to share the reproducer,
hopefully it yields something useful on your end.

I don't think this is Al's livelock, because only one thread is
spinning on an otherwise idle machine... but I apologize if it is and
it's going over my head.

Cheers,
Calvin

---
#!/usr/bin/python3
# sudo prlimit --nofile=10000000:10000000 ./repro.py

import os

NR_FORKS = 3

for _ in range(NR_FORKS):
	pid = os.fork()
	if pid == 0:
		break

for dirpath, _, filenames in os.walk("/proc"):
	for fn in filenames:
		try:
			os.open(os.path.join(dirpath, fn), os.O_RDONLY)
		except:
			pass

if pid == 0:
	os._exit(0)

while True:
	try:
		os.wait()
	except ChildProcessError:
		os._exit(0)

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro asked Calvin Owens to check if a specific Git repository branch reproduces the issue, indicating that he wants to verify whether the problem is consistent across different setups.

review_comments.author: Calvin Owens
review_comments.summary: Calvin Owens noted that the infinite spins issue is related to 'magic' symlinks in /proc/$/fd/*, and provided a patch (fs/proc: Implement d_weak_revalidate()) that makes it impossible to trigger the hang with his reproducer.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Al,

It still reproduces on that branch in the exact same way.

I'm absolutely 100% guessing... but I recall tomfoolery with the "magic"

---
From: Calvin Owens <calvin@wbinvd.org>
Subject: [PATCH] fs/proc: Implement d_weak_revalidate()

I think this is necessary because ND_JUMPED can happen in /proc when
the magic symlinks in /proc/$/fd/* point to files in /proc. If true,
this is a problem, because the default behavior for d_weak_revalidate()
is to assume the jumped-to dentries are always valid.

Fix by checking the in_use count in d_weak_revalidate() for procfs, the
same way it is checked in procfs d_revalidate() today.

Signed-off-by: Calvin Owens <calvin@wbinvd.org>
---
 fs/proc/generic.c | 22 +++++++++++++++++-----
 1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/proc/generic.c b/fs/proc/generic.c
index 501889856461..359176e5eeb3 100644
--- a/fs/proc/generic.c
+++ b/fs/proc/generic.c
@@ -216,15 +216,26 @@ void proc_free_inum(unsigned int inum)
 	ida_free(&proc_inum_ida, inum - PROC_DYNAMIC_FIRST);
 }
 
+static int __proc_misc_revalidate(struct dentry *dentry)
+{
+	if (atomic_read(&PDE(d_inode(dentry))->in_use) < 0)
+		return 0; /* revalidate */
+	return 1;
+}
+
 static int proc_misc_d_revalidate(struct inode *dir, const struct qstr *name,
 				  struct dentry *dentry, unsigned int flags)
 {
 	if (flags & LOOKUP_RCU)
 		return -ECHILD;
 
-	if (atomic_read(&PDE(d_inode(dentry))->in_use) < 0)
-		return 0; /* revalidate */
-	return 1;
+	return __proc_misc_revalidate(dentry);
+}
+
+static int proc_misc_d_weak_revalidate(struct dentry * dentry,
+				       unsigned int flags)
+{
+	return __proc_misc_revalidate(dentry);
 }
 
 static int proc_misc_d_delete(const struct dentry *dentry)
@@ -233,8 +244,9 @@ static int proc_misc_d_delete(const struct dentry *dentry)
 }
 
 static const struct dentry_operations proc_misc_dentry_ops = {
-	.d_revalidate	= proc_misc_d_revalidate,
-	.d_delete	= proc_misc_d_delete,
+	.d_revalidate		= proc_misc_d_revalidate,
+	.d_weak_revalidate	= proc_misc_d_weak_revalidate,
+	.d_delete		= proc_misc_d_delete,
 };
 
 /*
--

I appreciate you taking a look. I'm happy to test anything else you like
if that reproducer isn't working for you, it's very consistent for me on
two different machines. Hopefully it works for Jeff.

Thanks,
Calvin

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro demonstrated a pathological case where d_invalidate() gets stuck in an infinite loop due to a large number of busy dentries, and suggested that the patch should not use shrink_dcache_parent() in d_invalidate().

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC][PATCH] make sure that lock_for_kill() callers drop the locks in safe order
Re: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: kick writeback flusher instead of inline flush for IOCB_DONTCACHE

patches_acked.subject: Re: [RFC][PATCH] make sure that lock_for_kill() callers drop the locks in safe order
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch ensures that callers of the lock_for_kill() function properly drop locks in a safe order to prevent potential deadlocks or hangs.

review_comments.author: Paul McKenney
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that in a preempt-disabled kernel, the order of rcu_read_unlock() and spin_unlock() does not matter due to preemption being entirely disabled
sentiment_signals: no specific technical concern or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you, Jeff!

Yes that has preemption entirely disabled, so RCU readers do nothing but
disable and enable preemption.  Except that it is everywhere disabled,
so the order of the rcu_read_unlock() and the spin_unlock() should
not matter.  (Famous last words!)

							Thanx, Paul
